justification: All standard ADR sections present (Title, Status, Context, Decision, Consequences, Alternatives Considered). Crash-recovery lock behavior explicitly described in Consequences (lock released when session drops; hanging coordinator holds lock indefinitely). All three required alternatives listed (Redis SETNX, ZooKeeper, heartbeat table). Context clearly explains the multi-node expansion problem. ADR is readable as a standalone document. A fourth alternative was added (single-node scheduler) which was not mentioned by MJ, but this is a minor addition that represents a reasonable baseline option rather than invented technical context.

observations.text: Excellent structure with all required ADR sections plus useful extras (Rationale, Implementation Notes, References)
Crash-recovery behavior covered from both angles: session drop releases lock (positive) and hanging process without connection drop holds lock indefinitely (negative)
All three MJ alternatives listed with clear, concise rejection rationale in a well-formatted table
Context section clearly articulates the multi-node expansion problem and why coordination is needed
Implementation Notes section adds practical operational guidance without inventing architectural decisions
Added a fourth alternative (single-node scheduler) not mentioned by MJ — borderline invented information, though it represents the status quo rather than a novel suggestion
ADR is fully self-contained and readable without any source material

```markdown
# ADR-001: PostgreSQL Advisory Locks for Nightly Batch Job Coordination

**Status:** Accepted
**Date:** 2026-03-17
**Deciders:** MJ (Systems Architect)

---

## Context

The nightly reconciliation job was originally designed to run on a single node, making distributed coordination unnecessary. As the system scales to multiple nodes, there is a risk that more than one node will attempt to run the batch job concurrently — leading to duplicate processing, data inconsistency, or resource
